title: Schwab (SCHA) vs. iShares (IJR): Which Small Cap ETF Should Investors Buy?
author: Josh Kohn-Lindquist
description: A comparison of two popular small-cap ETFs reveals that while both offer low costs and similar long-term returns (~12.3-12.6% annualized), iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) is slightly favored due to its profitability requirements for holdings and more balanced diversification, whereas Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF (SCHA) has a concentrated position in SanDisk that has grown to 5% of the fund.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Offers lower expense ratio (0.04%) and broader diversification with 1,706 holdings, but has a concerning concentration risk with SanDisk representing 5% of the fund. No profitability requirements for holdings may pose higher risk during downturns.

Slightly favored choice due to profitability requirements for holdings (safer during recessions), more balanced sector distribution across 641 stocks, and comparable low costs and returns. Better suited for diversified small-cap exposure.

title: 3 Key Differences Between the Vanguard and iShares Small-Cap ETFs
author: Sarah Sidlow
description: The Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB) and iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) are compared across key metrics. VB offers a lower expense ratio (0.03% vs 0.06%), broader diversification with 1,300+ holdings, and higher dividend yield (1.20% vs 1.16%), while IJR provides more concentrated exposure with 640 holdings and delivered higher 1-year returns (31.5% vs 28.8%). Over five years, VB outperformed with $1,416 growth per $1,000 invested versus IJR's $1,321.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VB is highlighted for its lower expense ratio (0.03%), broader diversification with 1,300+ holdings, higher dividend yield (1.20%), larger AUM ($177.4B), and superior 5-year growth performance ($1,416 per $1,000 invested). These factors make it attractive for long-term investors seeking lower costs and better liquidity.

IJR is presented as a viable alternative with its own merits: higher 1-year returns (31.5%), more concentrated portfolio (640 holdings), and focus on higher-quality small caps with stronger financial viability requirements. However, it has higher fees (0.06%) and lower 5-year performance, making it neither clearly superior nor inferior, but rather a different strategic approach.

title: Small-Cap ETFs Are Beating The S&P 500 — But A Quality Problem May Be Building Underneath
author: Chandrima Sanyal
description: While small-cap stocks have outperformed the S&P 500 in 2026, Wells Fargo Investment Institute warns that the rally masks deteriorating fundamentals including declining earnings expectations, lower profitability, and elevated debt burdens. Investors are increasingly rotating toward quality-focused small-cap ETFs rather than broad-market exposure, as nearly 40% of Russell 2000 companies generate no earnings.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Experiencing over $8 billion in net outflows year-to-date; underlying Russell 2000 companies show declining earnings forecasts (-7%), with 40% generating no earnings and elevated debt-to-EBITDA ratios of 4.5x
Attracted approximately $2 billion in inflows; applies profitability screens to constituents, creating higher-quality portfolio composition that appeals to selective investors
Mentioned as a small-cap option but no specific performance data or flows provided; benefits from quality-focused investor rotation but exposed to broader small-cap weaknesses
Quality-oriented fund focused on stronger balance sheets and durable earnings; remains in investor focus as alternative to broad small-cap exposure
Quality-focused value fund attracting investor interest for stronger balance sheets; positioned to benefit from shift toward profitable small-cap companies

title: Small-Cap ETFs May Be Prepping For A Comeback Investors Are Still Ignoring: Here Are Four You Can Start With
author: Chandrima Sanyal
description: Small-cap stocks are beginning to outperform, but ETF investors remain focused on mega-cap technology and AI trades. Small-cap ETFs have declined from 10% to 4% of ETF industry assets despite the asset class regaining momentum. Strategists believe this disconnect could create an opportunity, as small caps trade at attractive valuations and could benefit from interest rate stabilization and broader economic growth.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Small-cap index showing early outperformance signs with potential for continued gains as investors rotate away from crowded mega-cap tech trades. Trading at attractive valuations relative to large-cap growth stocks.
Recommended as a higher-quality small-cap option with $2.1 billion in net inflows year-to-date, indicating growing investor interest in small-cap exposure.
Positioned as a broad-based diversification option for small-cap exposure with potential upside as market leadership broadens beyond mega-cap tech.
Recommended for growth-focused investors seeking faster-growing small-cap names; despite 9% returns, positioned to benefit from potential rotation trade.
Small-cap focused ETF attracting approximately $455 million in inflows, indicating growing institutional interest in the small-cap space.
Mega-cap tech-heavy fund currently receiving massive inflows but viewed as increasingly crowded; potential headwind if capital rotates to small caps.

title: Small-Cap ETF Showdown: Schwab's SCHA vs. iShares' IJR
author: Robert Izquierdo
description: The Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF (SCHA) and iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) offer different approaches to small-cap investing. SCHA provides broader diversification with 1,721 holdings and a lower 0.04% expense ratio, delivering higher 1-year returns (44% vs 37.1%), but carries higher volatility. IJR offers a more selective portfolio of 640 quality-screened holdings with lower risk and higher liquidity ($102.9B AUM), making it suitable for risk-conscious investors willing to pay a slightly higher 0.06% expense ratio.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: SCHA demonstrates superior 1-year returns (44% vs 37.1%), lowest expense ratio (0.04%), and broader market representation with 1,721 holdings, making it attractive for growth-focused investors seeking comprehensive small-cap exposure.

IJR offers lower volatility, reduced maximum drawdown (28% vs 30.8%), substantial AUM ($102.9B), quality-focused screening, and higher dividend yield (1.2%), making it suitable for risk-averse investors prioritizing stability.
Mentioned as a top holding in SCHA with recent positive price movement (+17.03%), but included only as a portfolio component without independent analysis or recommendation.
Listed as the largest position in IJR (0.74%), mentioned factually without independent sentiment or performance assessment.
